instruction:
Write a complete testthat test file for `rlang::arg_match`.

Package: rlang v1.1.7.9000
Description: Functions for Base Types and Core R and 'Tidyverse' Features
Function: arg_match
Parameters: arg, values, multiple, error_arg, error_call
Test patterns used: test_that
Expectations used: expect_equal, expect_snapshot

Function Code:
```r
arg_match <- function(
  arg,
  values = NULL,
  ...,
  multiple = FALSE,
  error_arg = caller_arg(arg),
  error_call = caller_env()
) {
  check_dots_empty0(...)

  arg_expr <- enexpr(arg)
  error_arg <- as_string(error_arg)

  check_symbol(arg_expr, arg = "arg")
  check_character(arg, arg = error_arg, call = error_call)

  if (is_null(values)) {
    fn <- caller_fn()
    values <- formals(fn)[[error_arg]]
    values <- eval_bare(values, get_env(fn))
  }

  if (multiple) {
    return(arg_match_multi(arg, values, error_arg, error_call))
  }

  if (!length(arg)) {
    msg <- sprintf(
      "%s must be length 1, not length 0",
      format_arg(error_arg)
    )
    abort(msg, call = error_call, arg = error_arg)
  }
  if (length(arg) > 1 && !setequal(arg, values)) {
    msg <- arg_match_invalid_msg(arg, values, error_arg)
    abort(msg, call = error_call, arg = error_arg)
  }

  arg <- arg[[1]]
  arg_match0(
    arg,
    values,
    error_arg,
    error_call = error_call
  )
}
```

Requirements:
1. Use test_that() blocks with descriptive names that reference `arg_match`
2. Test basic functionality using appropriate expectations (expect_equal, expect_snapshot)
3. Test edge cases: NULL input, empty strings, missing arguments
4. Test error conditions with expect_error()
5. Each test_that() block should test one logical behavior
